original_hypothesis.title: Factory application withdrawal can be forced to mature immediately, enabling instant deposit recycling/spam and operational DoS
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In AgentFactoryV2/V3/V4, proposeAgent sets `proposalEndBlock = block.number` (comment says no longer required). withdraw requires `block.number > proposalEndBlock`, which becomes true the very next block. This allows an attacker to rapidly propose and withdraw, cycling the applicationThreshold deposit with minimal capital lock, spamming NewApplication events and bloating operational processing/monitoring (and potentially any off-chain indexing/backends that process applications).
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete workflow defect: the maturity gate is effectively a 1-block delay, undermining any intended friction. Operationally, this can degrade UX, overwhelm off-chain pipelines, and create noisy state for any systems iterating applications.

proof_of_signal.pattern: proposalEndBlock = block.number; withdraw requires block.number > proposalEndBlock => withdrawable next block

preconditions: Attacker holds applicationThreshold amount of assetToken and can approve it to the factory

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Maturity condition exists but is effectively trivial; comment indicates deprecated behavior without removing guard
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Continuous, as long as attacker has capital and gas

operational_questions: Do any backends, bots, or operators treat NewApplication as a queue requiring manual review?
Is there any on-chain limit/rate-limit on applications per address or per block?
validation_requirements: Load test in fork: loop proposeAgent then withdraw next block and measure event throughput and any operational bottlenecks
Check for any external systems that iterate through application IDs and could be slowed by spam

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: In following block(s), call withdraw for each id to recycle funds
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Sustain spam rate to overwhelm monitoring/indexers or operational review

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Event/indexing DoS, noisy application state, potential operator fatigue/misprocessing
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Application lifecycle, off-chain processing, UI responsiveness

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time protocol is live and unpaused

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: High-frequency NewApplication events from a small set of addresses
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Indexer lag, backend queue growth, UI timeouts
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Propose/withdraw pairs occurring at ~1 block intervals

validation_result.mechanism: vestigial maturity gate enables application spam
validation_result.impact_summary: The proposalEndBlock is set to block.number at proposal time, and withdraw requires only block.number > proposalEndBlock (strict >), creating an effectively 1-block maturity. This allows rapid propose/withdraw cycling to spam NewApplication events with minimal capital lock.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis accurately identifies the code pattern across AgentFactoryV2, V3, and V4. In all three contracts, `proposeAgent` sets `proposalEndBlock = block.number` with the comment "No longer required in v2". The `withdraw` function retains the check `require(block.number > application.proposalEndBlock, "Application is not matured yet")`. Since proposalEndBlock equals the block at which the proposal was made, the withdrawal becomes possible in the very next block. This means an attacker can: (1) approve applicationThreshold to the factory, (2) call proposeAgent (locks tokens, emits NewApplication, increments _nextId), (3) in the next block call withdraw (gets tokens back), and (4) repeat indefinitely. The cost per cycle is only gas, as the applicationThreshold is fully recycled. The code retains an error message "Application is not matured yet" implying meaningful delay protection, but the actual delay is trivially 1 block (~2 seconds on Base L2). This is a real code defect: a maturity gate that provides no meaningful friction. While the comment indicates intentionality, the retained check with its misleading error message and the absence of any rate-limiting mechanism constitutes a design flaw that enables spam attacks against the protocol's application processing pipeline.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Direct evidence confirmed across all three factory contracts. The pattern is identical: proposalEndBlock = block.number in proposeAgent, and block.number > proposalEndBlock check in withdraw. The _nextId counter is unbounded and increments with each proposal, and NewApplication events are emitted without any rate limiting.

vulnerable_code_snippets.code_snippet:
uint256 id = _nextId++;
uint256 proposalEndBlock = block.number; // No longer required in v2
Application memory application = Application(
    name,
    symbol,
    tokenURI,
    ApplicationStatus.Active,
    applicationThreshold,
    sender,
    cores,
    proposalEndBlock,
    0,
    tbaSalt,
    tbaImplementation,
    daoVotingPeriod,
    daoThreshold
);
_applications[id] = application;
emit NewApplication(id);
vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: proposalEndBlock is set to block.number, making the maturity instant (withdrawable next block). Combined with the NewApplication event emission and _nextId increment, this enables rapid spam cycles.

The maturity check `block.number > application.proposalEndBlock` is satisfied in the very next block since proposalEndBlock = block.number at proposal time. The full applicationThreshold is returned, enabling zero-cost (minus gas) recycling.
Same pattern as V4 - proposalEndBlock = block.number with identical 1-block effective maturity.

vulnerable_code_snippets.code_snippet: require(block.number > application.proposalEndBlock, "Application is not matured yet");
vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Same vestigial maturity check as V4.

vulnerable_code_snippets.code_snippet: uint256 proposalEndBlock = block.number; // No longer required in v2

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker approves applicationThreshold of assetToken to factory
2. Attacker calls proposeAgent() - transfers applicationThreshold, sets proposalEndBlock = block.number, emits NewApplication, increments _nextId
3. In the next block (block.number + 1), attacker calls withdraw(id) - block.number > proposalEndBlock is satisfied (N+1 > N)
4. withdraw() transfers applicationThreshold back to attacker, sets status = Withdrawn
5. Attacker repeats from step 2 with recycled funds
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: proposeAgent sets proposalEndBlock = block.number (AgentFactoryV4.sol ~line 130). withdraw checks block.number > application.proposalEndBlock (AgentFactoryV4.sol ~line 160). Since proposalEndBlock is the current block at proposal time, the next block satisfies the strict inequality. The attacker recovers their full applicationThreshold via safeTransfer (line 168). Each cycle produces a NewApplication event and increments _nextId permanently.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment scripts (v2/deployFactory.ts, v2/deployVIPFactory.ts, v1/deployPersonaFactory.ts) deploy the factory contracts with applicationThreshold from environment variables but do not set any additional maturity/cooldown configuration. No rate limiting is configured.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack is straightforward and requires minimal technical skill: (1) Attacker holds applicationThreshold amount of assetToken, (2) Attacker approves factory, (3) Attacker calls proposeAgent in block N, (4) Attacker calls withdraw in block N+1, (5) Repeat. On Base L2 with ~2 second blocks, an attacker could create hundreds of spam applications per minute. The _nextId counter grows unboundedly, NewApplication events flood indexers, and the _applications mapping accumulates dead entries. The only cost is gas, which on Base L2 is very low.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The impact is primarily operational: (1) Unbounded growth of _nextId counter, (2) Accumulation of Withdrawn application entries in storage, (3) Flood of NewApplication events that can overwhelm off-chain indexers, monitoring systems, and UIs, (4) Potential operator fatigue if applications are manually reviewed. On-chain impact is limited to storage bloat and gas costs. No funds are at risk. The severity is correctly assessed as MEDIUM - it's a real code defect (vestigial maturity gate) that enables a spam vector, but doesn't threaten user funds directly.
recommended_mitigations: Either remove the proposalEndBlock check entirely (since the comment says it's no longer required) or implement a meaningful maturity delay (e.g., proposalEndBlock = block.number + maturityBlocks where maturityBlocks is configurable by admin)
Add a per-address cooldown between proposeAgent calls to prevent rapid cycling
Consider adding a per-address limit on active/total applications
If the maturity check is truly no longer needed, remove the vestigial code and the misleading error message to avoid confusion
validation_result.replication_plan: Deploy AgentFactoryV4 on a Base fork. Set applicationThreshold to a known value. Approve and call proposeAgent, then in next block call withdraw. Verify full fund recovery. Loop 100 times and measure: (1) total gas cost, (2) number of NewApplication events, (3) _nextId growth. Compare with off-chain indexer processing time to quantify DoS potential.
uncertainties: The actual applicationThreshold value in production is unknown from the code alone - if very high, the capital requirement may serve as a practical barrier
Base L2 gas costs may make high-frequency spam economically unattractive despite being technically feasible
Off-chain systems' resilience to event spam is unknown - impact depends on backend architecture
additional_testing_needed: Measure actual gas costs per propose/withdraw cycle on Base L2
Determine the applicationThreshold value used in production
Test off-chain indexer behavior under high NewApplication event throughput
Verify that withdrawn applications with incremented IDs don't cause issues in any on-chain iteration patterns
